review_text: This is a true horror, all the more disturbing for the apparent ease with which this couple accept the status quo. It also feels, sadly, incredibly relevant to the modern day.
Such a ghastly mental image and the professional compliments accompanying it come to sum up much of The Zone of Interest.

What makes the film beautiful also makes it deadly, going straight for the jugular when viewers least expect it and tackling the horrors which unfolded at Auschwitz and across concentration camps with reverence and respect.
The Zone of Interest is a brilliantly conceived, disturbingly disjointed film and it will stay with you long after it’s done.
You’ll remember Zone of Interest for the sheer shock of the narrative, the drab yet harrowing imagery and the brooding, creepy sounds of death heard through a concrete wall.
Thematically resonant, with significant things to say, but, at least for this reviewer, not quite satisfying in terms of an overall film experience – even taking into account the experimental nature.

Through sounds, details, and metaphors, the director manages to create a chilling atmosphere, giving the story the rawness and terror that a tale like this deserves. A discovery for finding a new way to tell a well-worn plot. [Full Review in Spanish]
Since Glazer’s entire approach is through its image and sound-making, you may even hear the sounds in your head long after the film has ended.

The Zone of Interest proves itself to be horrifically timely in how willingly we wall ourselves off from our complicity in genocide by any means necessary, however involved we are or how well-intentioned our actions may be.

The Zone of Interest...is easily one of the most disturbing movies you’ll ever see. Once again, [Jonathan Glazer] challenges us to look at films—and the world—from an unexpected and uncomfortable perspective.
What shocks in all of this is Rudolf and Hedwig’s dedication to their way of life and their seeming surety that this is simply the way it’s going to be.
The screenplay, co-written by the author of the book of the same title, Martin Amis, and Glazer, is well crafted and spare.
[Glazer's] resolutely human film makes abundantly clear where he stands against the banality of evil we must collectively resist.
The most terrifying and unsettling movie of the year.
An uncompromising and chilling vision of evil unlike any other film about Nazism.

Despite my feelings on this feature, the film itself is great and really emphasizes the cruelty of the Holocaust even though nothing technically happens on screen, a well-intentioned decision by the filmmakers.
Rather than show fascination even as a criticism of the central characters, Glazer renders the fascists as flat, dull objects.
The brutal hook of the film is in how it demonstrates the ease in which people, given the right circumstances, can become complicit in evil.
Glazer’s film terrifies not because of what’s on screen but because of what happens off screen.
t’s the banality of banality of banality, transacted beside the low and chatter of genocidal industry next door.

Once it arrives at its subversive, medium-bending conclusion, it’s already cemented its status as one of the toughest, most difficult historical dramas of its era — and one of the most distinctive works of the decade so far.
The question Glazer’s remarkable film raises in the viewer is, “What atrocities do our comfortable lives allow us to ignore?”
Hedwig and Rudolf are hardly the haunted Macbeths. The only damned spots that concern them are those on the poorly trimmed lilac bushes on the camp’s perimeter.

Glazer has delivered a profound and deeply unsettling masterpiece that serves as a timely reminder that, as it did in Nazi Germany, true evil continues to exist amid the mundane, and turning a blind eye is nothing less than complicity.

Presents a weird juxtaposition of mundane family issues against the Holocaust backdrop. It felt overly artistic—just because you can, doesn't mean you should. Artistically bold, but emotionally vacant.

While the deliberate indifference that is core to this film can be frustrating, Jonathan Glazer offers up his own and the pain of so many to put focus on those that keep going while calamity escalates.
Glazer wants us to reflect on what is normal, and what is abnormal, barbaric, obscene.
It is a harrowing experience realized through a brilliant motion picture.

By keeping concentration-camp obscenity off-screen, while referring to it obliquely through sci-fi sound effects and abstract visual metaphors, Glazer invites a new, smart-aleck sanctimony... Glazer has made an oddly complacent movie for an amoral era.
The film masterfully juxtaposes the serene family life of the couple with the harrowing reality of genocidal atrocities occurring just beyond their property fence.

Dedicate a decade of one's life on a conceptual film on the diabolical mechanics of the Holocaust, then craft it to appear as meticulously chilling and banal as its architects. [...] Jonathan Glazer is precisely that kind of courageous artist.

Jonathan Glazer turns us into witnesses of the most mundane, banal, and repugnant side of the Holocaust in a technical, narrative, and above all, hyper-realistic and discourse-driven masterpiece worthy of the most terrible nightmares.
Jonathan Glazer’s best film to date since Sexy Beast.
It is this unsettling overlay of routinely innocuous happenings contrasted by moments of total revulsion, that gives The Zone of Interest such an edge and makes it so disturbing.

The Zone of Interest is a misguided and disoriented work, one that conceals the concrete historical circumstances that produced someone like Höss, and therefore weakens the ability of the population today to defeat and destroy the fascist menace.
Glazer’s film is a punk provocation...

Glazer's masterstroke is to make The Zone of Interest seem like an easy watch — what could be unsettling about watching a family's daily rituals? — only to deliver one of the most unsettling and disturbing films you'll see.
The Zone of Interest follows a mostly realistic aesthetic, with a very clear linear narrative. Still, it's slightly experimental and subtly stylized.
As writer Dara Horn notes, that there seems to be no end to the number of stories about how Jews died, but very little interest in the ways in which they live.
A chilling story of murderous indifference...
It's an incredibly challenging film.

Glazer takes a meticulously forensic approach to the pair, but it could never be said he’s putting them under a microscope. Far from it. He’s determined to keep them at a distance, perhaps afraid that a single close-up might taint the whole exercise.

Despite some frustrations with the approach, I still recommend The Zone of Interest if only for the stunning sound design that becomes a character all its own and conveys the horror that lies just beyond the well groomed garden and its protective walls.
The Zone of Interest is a confronting sensory experience, but an incredibly powerful one.
The Zone of Interest is an exceptional film, and a difficult one to endure. The most vital stories are so often that way.
This movie shook me to my core, and if you can move past the achingly slow pace, it will for you, too.

With an observational look and feel, this intensely powerful film explores and exposes the ways in which people can live in parallel realities, ignoring the plight of others on the other side of the garden wall
Every scene has an eerie tone, an uneasy texture, an insistent backdrop of apocalypse.
This film recalls that harsh and heartbreaking phrase by Thomas Hobbes: "Man is man's wolf" and the wolf will always find justification to live with what he has done. [Full Review in Spanish]
After waiting for months to see this hard-to-get movie, I finally got to see it last night, and it is a good movie, but I don't think it lives up to its reputation.
